food_subgroup: Noodle dishes with protein and condiments

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for wonton noodles; closest parent 'noodle dish' (FOODON_03311069) used as anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for wonton noodles; A00LC (Pasta and noodles, cooked) used as closest parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for wonton noodles or sufficiently close noodle dish category.

main_ingredients: leafy vegetables (choy sum or kai lan)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of wantan mee is approximately 350 g, comprising a portion of egg noodles, three to four wonton dumplings, a few slices of char siu, and a side of blanched leafy greens.

nutrient_source.title: Wanton Noodles (Dry) — HPB Singapore Foodo / Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo lists 'Wanton Noodles (Dry)' as a distinct dish entry. Per-100g values are derived from the HPB Foodo database entry for this dish. The dish is also consistent with the FCID Singapore food composition data for mixed noodle dishes with pork and prawn wonton.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of egg noodles and mixed noodle dishes — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS et al. (2021) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load, Diabetes Care
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Singapore-style wantan mee as a composite dish. GI of 47 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for egg noodles (boiled, GI ~47, low category) as the dominant carbohydrate component, consistent with Atkinson et al. (2021) International Tables of GI and GL. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (47 × 75.25) / 100 ≈ 35.4, where carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 21.5 × 3.5 = 75.25 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~480 mg per 100 g; ~1680 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit wantan mee to occasional consumption and request reduced soy sauce and no added salt, as a single serving can contribute approximately 73% of the WHO recommended daily sodium limit of 2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~35 per serving) from refined egg noodles
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume wantan mee in controlled portions, preferring the soup version over the dry version to reduce fat and calorie density, and pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables to moderate the postprandial glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from char siu and lard, combined with high sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit wantan mee frequency due to its saturated fat content from char siu and cooking lard, and its high sodium load; choosing the soup variant with lean protein and requesting less oil can reduce cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Wantan Mee (云吞面 / 雲吞麵), also romanised as Wonton Mee or Wan Tan Mee, is a Cantonese-origin noodle dish that is a staple of Singaporean and Malaysian Chinese hawker cuisine. The dish consists of thin, springy egg noodles served either dry (tossed in a sauce of soy sauce, oyster sauce, sesame oil, and lard) or in a clear broth. It is typically accompanied by three to four boiled wonton dumplings filled with seasoned minced pork and/or prawn, slices of char siu (Cantonese barbecued pork), and blanched leafy greens such as choy sum or kai lan. The noodles have a firm, slightly chewy texture with a savoury, umami-rich flavour profile. In Singapore, the dish is commonly found at hawker centres and coffee shops and is priced affordably. Nutritionally, a standard 350 g serving provides approximately 518 kcal, 23.8 g protein, 14.7 g fat, and 75.3 g carbohydrate. The sodium content is notably high (~1680 mg per serving), making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index of the egg noodle base is approximately 47 (low GI), though the glycemic load per serving is moderate (~35) due to the large noodle portion. The dish contains gluten, egg, soy, and shellfish allergens and is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans due to the pork and prawn content.
common_user_queries: How many calories in wantan mee?
Is wantan mee suitable for diabetics?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo, which lists wanton noodles (dry) as a specific dish entry — the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database using egg noodles as the dominant carbohydrate component, which is a defensible analogous estimate consistent with peer-reviewed GI tables.
notes: 1. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source database for this dish. 2. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source database for this dish. 3. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source database for this dish. 4. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in this dish type per HPB data. 5. GI is an analogous estimate based on egg noodles (boiled) from the University of Sydney GI Database (GI ~47); no direct composite dish GI measurement was found in approved sources. 6. The input food_name '皇帝面 Wantan Mee' has been normalised to canonical English 'Wonton Noodles (Wantan Mee)'; '皇帝面' (Emperor Noodles) is a brand/restaurant name used by Hai Kah Lang and has been retained as an alias. 7. The dish is not certified halal as char siu typically uses pork; halal variants exist but are not the default. 8. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat values are estimated from component analysis consistent with HPB Foodo data for similar noodle dishes.
